original.question: Who is Arthas Menethil?
original.reference: Arthas Menethil was the Crown Prince of Lordaeron and a Knight of the Silver Hand. He became a death knight after claiming the cursed runeblade Frostmourne in Northrend, sacrificing his soul in the process. He eventually became the Lich King after merging with Ner'zhul, and ruled from the Frozen Throne in Icecrown Citadel until he was defeated by a group of adventurers.
snippets: Arthas Menethil: Arthas Menethil, Crown Prince of Lordaeron and Knight of the Silver Hand, was the son of King Terenas Menethil II and heir to the throne. He was trained as a paladin by Uther the Lightbringer and was inducted into the Order of the Silver Hand. Arthas also had a romantic relationship with the kind sorceress Jaina Proudmoore. Committed to the protection of his people, Arthas was determined to stop the plague spreading throughout Lordaeron. Despite his promising beginnings, Arthas became one of the most powerful and evil beings Azeroth would ever know. Although he killed the necromancer Kel'Thuzad, Arthas was unable to stop the spread of the plague and resorted to increasingly desperate methods, such as purging the city of Stratholme. Taking up the cursed runeblade Frostmourne, Arthas was able to defeat the demon Mal'Ganis, but he lost his soul in the process. Becoming a death knight of the Scourge, he led the undead in destroying Lordaeron, Quel'Thalas, and Dalaran. Arthas later made his way to the Frozen Throne of Icecrown and merged with the Lich King.March Badness Ruling as the Lich King for years afterward, Arthas had the last remnants of his humanity destroyed and was ultimately defeated in combat by Highlord Tirion Fordring and his champions of the Light. Cradled by the spirit of his father, Arthas Menethil died, leaving the mantle of the Lich King to be taken by a noble soul who would contain the power of the Scourge.

Echo of Arthas Menethil: The Echo of Arthas Menethil is being tortured by the Echo of Ner'zhul inside Frostmourne during The Call of Icecrown. __NOTOC__ Abilities Quotes Trivia The Echo of Arthas Menethil is voiced by Patrick Seitz. The Menethils (sometimes called the Menethil Dynasty)The Last Menethil were the ruling family of the Kingdom of Lordaeron prior to the nation's destruction and the murder of King Terenas Menethil II by his son, Prince Arthas at the start of the Third War. The house was known for their kindness to the Wrynns during the Second War, when the Horde ruled over the lands of Stormwind. The Menethils ruled for many decades, with Terenas' reign alone lasting 50 years.Arthas: Rise of the Lich King, chapter 17 Terenas's grandfather is said to have had successful negotiations with the elves of Quel'Thalas over the borders there for his northern vassals.Day of the Dragon, pg. 82 As of the Fourth War and beyond, the royal Menethil bloodline is all but extinct, with the possible exception of Calia Menethil's sole surviving daughter, and Calia herself has thus far expressed very limited interest in becoming ruler of Lordaeron, both in life and in her current state.

original.response:
# Arthas Menethil

**Arthas Menethil was the Crown Prince of Lordaeron who became one of Azeroth's greatest villains.** He began as a promising paladin trained by Uther the Lightbringer and member of the Order of the Silver Hand, deeply committed to protecting his people. He even had a romantic relationship with the sorceress Jaina Proudmoore and showed genuine heroism in his early years.

However, his descent into darkness was swift and complete. When a devastating plague spread through Lordaeron, Arthas became increasingly desperate to stop it. After killing the necromancer Kel'Thuzad proved insufficient, he resorted to horrific measures, including the purging of the city of Stratholme. He took up the cursed runeblade **Frostmourne** to defeat the demon Mal'Ganis, but at a terrible cost—he lost his soul in the process and became a death knight of the Scourge.

**As the Lich King's servant, Arthas led the undead in destroying Lordaeron, Quel'Thalas, and Dalaran.** He eventually made his way to Icecrown and merged with the Lich King himself, ruling as this merged entity for years while the last traces of his humanity were stripped away. He was ultimately defeated in combat by Highlord Tirion Fordring and his champions, and died cradled by the spirit of his father, King Terenas Menethil II.

original.question: What is Frostmourne?
original.reference: Frostmourne is a cursed runeblade created by the Lich King. It was used by Arthas Menethil after he claimed it in Northrend, which caused him to lose his soul and become a death knight. The sword has the power to steal the souls of those it kills. It was eventually shattered during the final battle against the Lich King in Icecrown Citadel.
snippets: Frostmourne: thumb|upright=0.8|The Lich King holding Frostmourne. Frostmourne was the mourneblade of the Lich King. A fearsome, rune-inscribed two-handed longsword,Blood of the Highborne, chapter 2 it possessed the power to shatterA Paladin's Soul and imprison the souls of its victims and could remake the living into mindless undead. Frostmourne was thrust from the Frozen Throne by the Lich King Ner'zhul and was taken up by Arthas Menethil in the wastes of Northrend during the Third War. It was wielded by Arthas both during his time as a death knight and as Lich King until it was destroyed by Tirion Fordring; in that time, the blade spilled the blood of kings, destroyed nations, and inflicted scars on the world that remain to this day. Few weapons have shaped modern history as much as Frostmourne.Libram of the Dead: Blades of the Fallen Prince During the third invasion of the Burning Legion, a death knight hero reforged the shards of the blade into the Blades of the Fallen Prince. History Origin thumb|A vision of Frostmourne in the hands of the Jailer. Frostmourne and the Helm of Domination were crafted at the Runecarver's Oubliette in Torghast by the Runecarver, who was forced against his will to do so by the Jailer.

Frostmourne Cavern: thumb|Frostmourne Cavern in World of Warcraft. thumb|Arthas Menethil leaving the unconscious Muradin Bronzebeard behind in Frostmourne Cavern. Frostmourne Cavern is a cave located at the base of the Icecrown Glacier where the dark runeblade Frostmourne once lay on an altar, encased in ice, with the following inscription: "Whomsoever takes up this blade shall wield power eternal. Just as the blade rends flesh, so must power scar the spirit". The ice-rimmed cavern that extends back into the mountains is full of saronite.Exploring Azeroth: Northrend, pg. 91 The cavern was given its name by the Scarlet Onslaught.Frostmourne Cavern (quest) At the time of the Third War, its entrance was protected by a group of revenants led by one of them known as The Guardian.Frostmourne (WC3 Human) When Arthas Menethil came with Muradin Bronzebeard to claim the artifact in his quest of destroying the dreadlord Mal'Ganis, the Guardian ordered them to turn back, and when they refused, a fight took place and all the revenants were destroyed. In its final moments, the Guardian attempted to stop Arthas, explaining that he was not protecting the sword, but everyone from it. However, the prince didn't listen and claimed Frostmourne from its ice prison, which caused its explosion, and a piece of it "killed" Muradin.

original.response:
# Frostmourne: The Blade That Shaped Azeroth's Fate

**Frostmourne** is one of World of Warcraft's most iconic and consequential weapons—a rune-inscribed two-handed longsword that belonged to the Lich King. This mourneblade possessed terrifying power: it could shatter souls, imprison the souls of those it struck, and transform the living into mindless undead servants. The blade was crafted by the Runecarver in Torghast against his will, forced by the Jailer to create both Frostmourne and the Helm of Domination.

The sword's most pivotal chapter began when **Arthas Menethil** claimed it from the Frostmourne Cavern at the base of Icecrown Glacier during the Third War. The inscription on its altar warned: "Whomsoever takes up this blade shall wield power eternal. Just as the blade rends flesh, so must power scar the spirit." Taking the blade sealed Arthas's dark fate—it was a trap that bound him to the Lich King's will. Arthas wielded Frostmourne throughout his campaign as a death knight and later as the Lich King himself, and the blade spilled the blood of kings, destroyed entire nations, and left scars on the world that endure to this day.

**Tirion Fordring** finally destroyed Frostmourne during the Lich King's defeat. The shards were later reforged into the **Blades of the Fallen Prince** by a death knight hero during the Legion's third invasion.

original.question: What is the Wrathgate?
original.reference: The Wrathgate, also known as Angrathar the Wrathgate, is a massive iron gate that serves as the entrance to Icecrown from the Dragonblight in Northrend. It was the site of a major battle where Horde and Alliance forces fought against the Scourge. The battle was interrupted when a faction of undead called the Forsaken Royal Apothecary Society unleashed a plague that killed both Scourge and Alliance and Horde forces, including High Overlord Saurfang the Younger.
snippets: Angrathar the Wrathgate: thumb|upright=1.1|Angrathar the Wrathgate. thumb|upright=1.1|After being cleaned by the red dragonflight. Angrathar the Wrathgate is a barred entrance to Icecrown Citadel and is located in the northwest of Dragonblight. It is a towering wall of knife edges made from saronite. During the war against the Lich King, the battle at the Wrath Gate was to be the first major blow against the Lich King, and the first in which the Alliance and Horde fought side by side instead of against each other. History Wrath of the Lich King thumb|Warlord Dranosh Saurfang and Highlord Bolvar Fordragon fighting the undead at the Wrathgate. During the war against the Lich King, the Wrath Gate was under siege by both the Alliance and the Horde forces, which set up the bases of Fordragon Hold and Kor'kron Vanguard. Respectively led by Highlord Bolvar Fordragon and Warlord Dranosh Saurfang, their forces intended to defeat the Scourge forces at the gate and to storm into Icecrown Citadel. Their campaign culminated in the disastrous Battle of Angrathar the Wrathgate, where they faced the Lich King himself, before being betrayed by Grand Apothecary Putress and his allies, who betrayed the Horde and all present by unleashing the New Plague on all the gathered forces. As the plague spread through the battlefield, many Horde and Alliance attempted to flee. The Lich King was also in serious danger of succumbing to the plague, and only barely managed to escape back through the Wrathgate.

Chronocrystal - Memory of the Wrathgate: thumb|Chronocrystal - Memory of the Wrathgate The Chronocrystal - Memory of the Wrathgate is a Chronocrystal located in the Caverns of Time next to Sa'at. This crystal plays The Wrathgate. Patch changes External links Category:Crystals Category:Caverns of Time objects

# The Wrathgate

The **Wrathgate** is a massive barred entrance to Icecrown Citadel located in northwestern Dragonblight. It's constructed from towering walls of saronite with knife-like edges, serving as the primary gateway to the Lich King's stronghold.

During the **Wrath of the Lich King expansion**, the Wrathgate became the site of a historic battle where the Alliance and Horde fought together for the first time as allies against the Scourge. Led by Highlord Bolvar Fordragon and Warlord Dranosh Saurfang respectively, their combined forces laid siege to the gate with the goal of breaching Icecrown Citadel and striking a major blow against the Lich King himself.

However, the **Battle of Angrathar the Wrathgate** ended in catastrophic failure. As the combined forces pushed toward victory, Grand Apothecary Putress betrayed everyone by unleashing the devastating New Plague across the entire battlefield. The plague killed soldiers indiscriminately, forcing both Alliance and Horde to retreat in chaos. Even the Lich King was nearly killed by the plague and barely managed to escape back through the gate. This devastating betrayal marked a turning point in the war against the Lich King, shifting the conflict in unexpected directions.

original.question: Who is Sylvanas Windrunner?
original.reference: Sylvanas Windrunner is a former Ranger-General of Silvermoon who was killed by Arthas Menethil and raised as a banshee. She eventually regained her body and became the leader of the Forsaken undead faction. She later became Warchief of the Horde after Garrosh Hellscream and Vol'jin. She is known for burning Teldrassil and for her role in the Shadowlands storyline where she shattered the Helm of Domination.
snippets: Sylvanas Windrunner: Lady Sylvanas Windrunner, formerly styled "the Dark Lady" and "the Banshee Queen", is the former Warchief of the Horde and former supreme ruler of the Forsaken, one of the most powerful factions of undead on Azeroth. In life, Sylvanas was the ranger-general of Silvermoon, whose leadership acumen and martial prowess were without equal. During the Third War, she bravely defended Quel'Thalas from a Scourge invasion led by the death knight Arthas Menethil. Ultimately, however, Sylvanas fell in battle. Rather than honor the ranger-general with a quick death, Arthas ripped out her soul and transformed it into a banshee: a cunning and vengeful agent of the Lich King empowered by hate. When the Lich King's control over his minions weakened, Sylvanas broke away from her tyrannical master's control and reclaimed her body. Vowing to avenge her death, Sylvanas gathered other renegade undead and set out to wage war against the Scourge. Thus it was that the Forsaken came to be, with Sylvanas as their queen. Under Sylvanas' command, the Forsaken joined the Horde and later helped bring about the Lich King's fall in the frozen wastes of Northrend. She seeks a higher purpose for a people who have already died once.The Characters of Warcraft/Sylvanas Windrunner Yet many challenges still lay ahead for Sylvanas. After an uprising within her ranks that killed other members of the Horde, the banshee queen is now mistrusted by many of her allies.

Sylvanas Windrunner (tactics): Sylvanas Windrunner is the final boss of the Sanctum of Domination. The encounter is an epic battle that takes its start at the Pinnacle of Domination before continuing across the Edge of the Abyss into the Crucible in Oribos, all while Sylvanas tries to waylay the heroes of Azeroth and prevent them from interfering with the Jailer's plan. Adventure Guide With Oribos in peril, a desperate battle at the pinnacle of the Sanctum begins. Standing between the Jailer and the mortals who would stop him is Sylvanas Windrunner. With no masks left to wear, the Banshee Queen unleashes her full power to protect the Banished One while his ultimate plan unfolds. Overview Azeroth's champions battle Sylvanas at the pinnacle of the Sanctum of Domination, across the vastness between the Maw and the Eternal City, and within the Arbiter's chamber. Players first encounter Sylvanas atop Torghast. Upon reaching 85% health, she rips apart chunks of the tower itself. As the Jailer envelops Oribos in the Maw, players pursue him with the aid of Thrall, Jaina Proudmoore, and Bolvar Fordragon, forging new paths and defeating Mawforged commanders while Sylvanas harrows from all sides in Banshee Form. Within the Crucible, the Banished One executes his grand plan. Our allies confront the dominated Anduin Wrynn while Sylvanas engages players directly. Damage Dealers – While under the effects of Windrunner, spells and abilities strike Sylvanas where she cast Windrunner.

Several members of the family were thought to be killed by the orcs during the Second War, including the sisters' parents, their youngest brother Lirath, along with numerous relatives of varying proximity.Warcraft II: Beyond the Dark Portal manual, Legends of the Land, AlleriaBeyond the Dark Portal, pg. 68 Lireesa as the ranger-general of Silvermoon died during the attack, and Sylvanas became the new ranger-general, but gave :Thas'dorah, Legacy of the Windrunners to her older sister, Alleria, who was a distinguished Farstrider. Alleria would wield Thas'dorah in battle against the Legion for centuries before it was lost on Niskara and eventually retrieved by a hunter and her younger sister Vereesa.Tales of the Hunt Only Alleria, Sylvanas, Vereesa, and Zendarin are known to have survived.
original.response:
# Sylvanas Windrunner

**Sylvanas Windrunner is one of World of Warcraft's most iconic and complex characters—a former high elf ranger-general who was transformed into an undead banshee and became the supreme leader of the Forsaken faction.** In life, she served as Ranger-General of Silvermoon and defended Quel'Thalas with unmatched martial skill. During the Third War, the death knight Arthas invaded her homeland, and rather than grant her a honorable death, he ripped out her soul and twisted it into a vengeful banshee under the Lich King's control.

When the Lich King's power weakened, Sylvanas broke free from his tyranny and reclaimed her body. Vowing vengeance against the Scourge, she gathered other renegade undead and founded **the Forsaken**, leading them to join the Horde. Under her command, the Forsaken became a major force on Azeroth and played a crucial role in the Lich King's eventual defeat in Northrend. She held the title of Warchief of the Horde at her height of power.

However, Sylvanas's reign became increasingly controversial. After an uprising within her own ranks that killed other Horde members, she lost the trust of many of her allies. Her story reached a dramatic climax when she served as the final boss of the Sanctum of Domination, standing between the heroes of Azeroth and the Jailer's ultimate plan—a confrontation that stripped away all her masks and forced her to unleash her full power.

original.question: What is the Lich King?
original.reference: The Lich King is the ruler of the undead Scourge and resides on the Frozen Throne in Icecrown Citadel in Northrend. The original Lich King was Ner'zhul, an orc shaman whose spirit was trapped in armor by Kil'jaeden. Arthas Menethil later merged with Ner'zhul to become the new Lich King. After Arthas was defeated, Bolvar Fordragon became the new Lich King to keep the Scourge in check.
snippets: Lich King: While the Lich King dreamt, the various personas in his mind - the death knight Arthas Menethil, the orc shaman Ner'zhul, and Matthias Lehner, the personification of the remnants of Arthas's humanity - fought for influence and control over the entity. Matthias tried to reason with Arthas, but Arthas silenced him by running him through with Frostmourne. Ner'zhul was delighted by this, declaring that he and Arthas were now free to merge into a single glorious being. Arthas rejected this offer, stating that once he had the power of the Lich King, no one would tell him what to do again. He impaled the stunned Ner'zhul with Frostmourne, becoming the dominant personality of the Lich King and ending the dream.Arthas: Rise of the Lich King, Epilogue In his quiet meditation, the part of the Lich King that was Arthas began to reflect on the events of his life, on the path that had taken him so far from the young boy who who had been his father's pride and joy. In the midst of his reflections, Arthas sensed a bitter and ancient presence locked within the nearby ice, and with this realization came the name Sindragosa.The Cinematic Art of World of Warcraft: Wrath of the Lich King Wrath of the Lich King Awakening thumb|upright=1.2|The awakened Lich King and his undead armies in Icecrown.

Lich King (tactics): The Lich King is the final boss of Icecrown Citadel and the final major encounter of World of Warcraft: Wrath of the Lich King. This boss has been available since February 2, 2010 on US servers and February 3 on EU servers. The Lich King is a long and challenging battle for survival. All classes are tested to the limits of their abilities, in particular all raid members have to react quickly to various abilities, often several at the same time. The encounter is rather unforgiving; most players must react properly in various situations, and all players get ample opportunity to commit mistakes which then wipe the raid. This encounter is a fitting end to Wrath of the Lich King and on par with Yogg-Saron in Ulduar. Abilities The Lich King melees for roughly 40,000 on an item level 264 geared tank on 25-player difficulty. The Lich King enrages after 15 minutes of combat, gaining 900% damage and 150% attack speed. However, he is notably still affected by taunt abilities. Phase 1 Phase 1.5 and 2.5 Phase 2 The Lich King retains Infest. Phase 3 The Lich King retains Soul Reaper and Defile. Frostmourne Spirit Warden Terenas Menethil Phase 4 Terenas Menethil Preparation The fight requires the standard raid composition of 2/3 Tanks and 3/5 healers (10/25 player), while learning the encounter more healers can help. No resistance or other special gear is required.

Icon of the Lich King: thumb|250px|Icon of the Lich King. The Icon of the Lich King is the sigil of the Scourge. Its azure coloration and icy ornamentation symbolize the frigid homeland of the undead horde in Northrend. At the icon's center is the now-shattered runeblade Frostmourne, the former blade of the Lich King Arthas Menethil. The frost-covered hammers flanking the blade represent the Scourge's endless capacity for warfare, as well as their construction of the saronite citadel Icecrown and the dread Necropoli that shepherded the endless armies of the dead and served as hubs of unspeakable torment upon the living. Although the holder of the Helm of Domination has changed since the creation of the sigil, and the goals of the Scourge along with them, this sigil seems to have remained representative of the organization. The Cult of the Damned, an affiliate group consisting primarily of the living, retains both a unique icon and the twisted ambitions of the original Scourge. Gallery See also Icon Category:Icons

The Birth of the Lich King: "The Birth of the Lich King" is a section of the History of Warcraft, Chapter IV. It is set immediately following the events of Warcraft II: Beyond the Dark Portal. Found at: Reliquary of Scholomance in the Western Plaguelands Southshore Inn in the Hillsbrad Foothills The Scarlet Bastion (live side) of Stratholme The Darkshire town hall in Duskwood, next to Lord Ello Ebonlocke Timeline: ← Previous book: The Invasion of Draenor → Next book: Icecrown and the Frozen Throne Further reading Ner'zhul's escape is detailed in the novel Beyond the Dark Portal. Notes Typo "the Azeroth" is not found in the game but remained in the site's version. This updated version originally comes from the Warcraft III manual chapter titled Kil'jaeden and the New Deal. Herald of the Lich King: thumb|The Herald in The Winds of War The Herald of the Lich King was an unseen entity that did a series of zone-wide yells during the second Scourge Invasion. In the comic, when the Scourge attacked Stormwind Harbor and Orgrimmar, a large, shade-like figure speaks similar lines.The Winds of War Quotes Wrath of the Lich King Random Stormwind Harbor Ring of Valor, interrupting Thrall and Garrosh's mak'gora Invasion ends Comic Alliance "Hear me, foolish children of the Alliance! Today, we will forge a new alliance--for today, you will fight and die! And your bodies will rise to join the Lich King's undead army! All hail the Lich King...soon to be king of Stormwind...and all of Azeroth! Horde "Hear me, Orcs of Orgrimmar! Brash upstarts of the Horde! Tremble and know your doom, for the Lich King's gaze is fixed upon you!" Notes During the first Scourge Invasion, the Mouth of Kel'Thuzad yelled the lines. original.response:
# The Lich King

**The Lich King is the supreme ruler of the Scourge**, an undead army that threatened all of Azeroth. The entity itself is a composite being formed from multiple consciousnesses merged together—most notably the death knight Arthas Menethil, the orc shaman Ner'zhul, and the remnants of Arthas's humanity personified as Matthias Lehner.

**Arthas became the dominant personality** after a bitter internal struggle during the Lich King's dream state. When Ner'zhul offered to merge with Arthas into a single being, Arthas rejected him, impaling both Ner'zhul and Matthias with Frostmourne to ensure absolute control. Arthas's victory meant no one would ever tell him what to do again—he would wield the Lich King's power on his own terms.

**As the final boss of Icecrown Citadel**, the Lich King represented the climactic confrontation of Wrath of the Lich King. The battle tested all raid members severely, requiring perfect coordination against his devastating abilities across multiple phases. His power was symbolized by the Icon of the Lich King—featuring the shattered runeblade Frostmourne at its center, flanked by frost-covered hammers representing the Scourge's endless capacity for warfare and construction of their Saronite fortress.

original.question: What is Illidan Stormrage known for?
original.reference: Illidan Stormrage is a night elf demon hunter and the twin brother of Malfurion Stormrage. He is known for drinking from the Skull of Gul'dan which transformed him into a demon-like being. He was imprisoned for ten thousand years for creating a new Well of Eternity. After being freed he took control of Outland and the Black Temple. He is often associated with the quote 'You are not prepared.' He was later revealed to be a crucial weapon against the Burning Legion.
snippets: Illidan Stormrage: While Malfurion had spent a lifetime fighting for the dream of what Azeroth could be, he now must fight for what it is. With his final words, Illidan asked Malfurion to take care of Tyrande and listen to her counsel, for she was always the best of them. The road ahead would be long, but whatever came, he knew Malfurion would bring honor to the name Stormrage. To Tyrande, he spoke of how all the paths he had taken had led to a single purpose, saving the world; he could bide no half measures or compromises. He told her how whenever he faced doubt, he held on to one anchor: Her. He told Tyrande that she embodied the best of Azeroth with her faith and devotion, and asked her to take care of Malfurion. While he sometimes wished her heart had made a different choice, in the end he knew she made the right one. Atop Hyjal, on the shores of the Well of Eternity that he created ages ago, Illidan told the adventurer that they had proven their commitment to Azeroth, that their dedication and sacrifice rivaled his own.

The Hunt for Illidan Stormrage: The Hunt for Illidan Stormrage is the continuation of the Illidan Stormrage campaign in Patch 7.1.0. Objectives Seek out Allari the Souleater at Illidari Stand in Azsuna. Description Allari the Souleater, an oracle of the Illidari, has been on the hunt for Illidan's soul for quite some time. She now believes that she has discovered its location and requires your assistance in its recovery. Find Allari at Illidari Stand in Azsuna and aid her in any way possible. If Sargeras truly plans to use Illidan's body as a vessel for his own return then returning Illidan's soul to his body is integral in preventing the dark titan from returning to our world! Rewards You will receive: Completion The naaru heard my call? Interesting... Very well, let's get to work. We've found Illidan's soul - finally - and it's not where we expected it to be held. Progression Patch changes External links

Illidan Stormrage (tactics): Illidan Stormrage is the final boss of the Black Temple. The fight consists of five phases, and good positioning is the key to success. Biography Adventure Guide Overview Illidan wields his Warglaives at the start of the encounter, infecting foes with Parasitic Shadowfiend. Upon reaching 65% health, Illidan takes flight and throws the Warglaives of Azzinoth to the ground, each of which unleashes a Flame. The two Flames of Azzinoth must be defeated to bring Illidan to the ground. For the remainder of the encounter, Illidan periodically unleashes the power of the demonic force within him, using those dark powers to assault his foes, before returning to his natural form. At 30% health, Maiev arrives to assist in handling the Betrayer's final efforts to overpower his foes. file:Dps_icon.png Damage Dealers Move away from allies when afflicted with Parasitic Shadowfiend. Quickly defeat the Flames of Azzinoth in Stage Two of the encounter. file:Healer_icon.png Healers Move away from allies when afflicted with Parasitic Shadowfiend. Quickly defeat the Flames of Azzinoth in Stage Two of the encounter. file:Tank_icon.png Tanks Use Active Mitigation defensive abilities to survive Shear. Move Illidan Stormrage to avoid damage taken from Flame Crash. In Stage Two, be quick to grab the attention of the Flames of Azzinoth.

Lord Illidan Stormrage (quest): Lord Illidan Stormrage is the second of the three storyline quests, available starting at reputation with Netherwing. Objectives Meet Illidan Stormrage. Seek out Barthamus in the Lower City of Shattrath should you survive. You will need: Meeting with Illidan Stormrage Description Come, highlord . Illidan himself awaits us! Completion I was the first, you know. The abandoned child of a monster... We owe you everything, . You have single-handedly shattered the Dragonmaw empire and gathered enough information to keep my brethren occupied for ten lifetimes. It is not much but there is something that we can offer... My kin have each offered to join you on your mission in Outland. Simply ask and they will bond with you. Quest Dialogue During this quest, Overlord Mor'ghor summons an image of Illidan at the Dragonmaw Base Camp to meet the "Dragonmaw orc" (the player in disguise) who will be their champion: An image of Illidan appears. Illidan immobilizes you. Illidan's image vanishes. Yarzill the Merc transforms into a nether dragon! Notes thumb|Riding off into the sunset on Yarzill's back At the end of the dialogue sequence, Yarzill transforms into a nether dragon and carries the player to Shattrath City. The trip is slow, as Yarzill flies at about walking speed. It is advised to have the necessary time before triggering this quest, although as of patch 2.3.0 the speed has been raised.

Awakenings: Objectives Witness the Awakening of Illidan Stormrage in Val'sharah. Witness the Awakening of Illidan Stormrage Description Light's Heart is to serve as the vessel for Illidan Stormrage's rebirth: my last act of service to the Light. But Illidan is dead and his soul is lost in the Nether. Before we may attempt to recover his soul, we must prepare the vessel for his return. Across two worlds exist echoes of Illidan's past. You must venture out and find the echoes. Start where all life begins, : birth. Find Illidan's birthplace in Val'sharah. Awaken the memory. Rewards You will receive: Progress When our work is complete, I will be lost to the Great Dark. Completion An auspicious start to a life of tragedy. Perhaps one of the few joyous memories Illidan would have in his life. While the years that followed tested the prophesied child, they would never break him. You must remember that, despite what you may see as our journey continues. There is much more to be done, but I am not yet ready. I will call for you when it is time. Gains 20,560 XP Notes Go to Lorlathil in Val'sharah. This will prompt a message from Xe'ra. Press the extra action button to see the cinematic. thumb|Elves in front of the newborn Stormrage twins. A crowd of night elves has assembled in Lorlathil around two cribs, presided by a lead Priestess of the Moon wearing the Raiment of Divine Clarity set, and six more priestesses each wearing a Mooncloth Robe.
original.response:
# Illidan Stormrage

**Illidan is known as one of the most pivotal and tragic figures in Warcraft history.** He's primarily remembered as a demon hunter and sorcerer of immense power, best known for his role as the final boss of the Black Temple during The Burning Crusade expansion. As the "Betrayer," Illidan became a complex character—neither purely evil nor good—whose actions shaped the fate of Azeroth itself.

**His greatest accomplishment was creating the Well of Eternity ages ago**, and his life's purpose ultimately centered on saving the world, even if it required extreme measures and sacrifices. He's also renowned for wielding the Warglaives of Azzinoth and his demonic powers. Despite his fearsome reputation, Illidan harbored deep love for Tyrande Whisperwind and respect for his brother Malfurion, holding onto those relationships as anchors through his turbulent existence.

**In later storylines, Illidan's soul becomes central to preventing the dark titan Sargeras from returning to Azeroth**, making him crucial to the world's defense even beyond death. His legacy is one of dedication and sacrifice—a being who walked dark paths but always toward the singular goal of protecting his world, demanding no half measures or compromises in that mission.

original.question: What are Mystics in Warcraft III and where do you encounter them?
original.reference: Mystics were night elf units in Warcraft III: Reign of Chaos that appeared exclusively in the night elf mission Brothers in Blood. They were part of the Barrow Deeps Watchers and resided in the prison's library, where they guarded magical artifacts. Notable for casting shamanistic spells, they were the only non-druid male night elves in the game.

They were part of the Barrow Deeps Watchers, and resided only in the prison's library guarding a number of magical artifacts. They have the appearance of human Apprentice Wizards, a variant of the high elf Priests, and are tinted red. They notably cast shamanistic spells and are the only non-druid male night elves in the game. Deals 400 damage to summoned units. Duration (Hero) 15 (5) sec. Bloodlust While Mystics do technically have the Bloodlust ability, it is the Shaman's version, not the neutral version. They do not have it researched, so they can't actually use it. Notes In Reforged, they look like high elves. original.response:
# Mystics in Warcraft III

**Mystics were night elf units exclusive to the mission "Brothers in Blood"** in Warcraft III: Reign of Chaos. They served as part of the Barrow Deeps Watchers, a group tasked with guarding magical artifacts in a prison's library. These units had a distinctive appearance — they looked like human Apprentice Wizards tinted red, making them visually unique among night elf forces.

**Combat-wise, Mystics were spellcasters with a shamanistic bent.** Their main ability was Purge, which removed all buffs from a target unit and severely slowed its movement speed by 80% for 15 seconds before gradually recovering. They could also theoretically access Bloodlust, but it wasn't researched during gameplay. What made Mystics particularly notable was that they were **the only non-druid male night elves in the entire game** — a rare exception to the game's typical character designs.

In Warcraft III: Reforged, Mystics received a visual update and now resemble high elves instead of their original appearance. Lore-wise, they're often compared to night elf lorekeepers, sharing the trait of being hidden knowledge-keepers in underground locations.

Banshee (Warcraft III): Banshees were once beautiful female Night Elves who were brutally murdered by demons during the fall of Kalimdor. Their restless spirits were left to wander the world for many ages in silent, tortured lamentation. Over the years they became jealous of the living and seeded a deep hatred for all life. Yet when the Lich King, Ner'zhul, became aware of them, he gathered them together and promised them vengeance upon the living. Ner'zhul gave them terrible voices so that the living would finally hear their timeless anguish. Thus, the Banshees have pledged themselves to the Undead cause and serve as valuable warriors in the Scourge. The spectral Banshees can fly and are fond of hammering their enemies with tortured blasts of sonic force. Their unnerving, unholy screams can be heard from miles away. Information Banshees are very good at supporting units, coming with their initial spell of Curse and can later learn Anti-Magic Shell and Possession. They're still fairly weak like Necromancers and need protection. Banshees are the bane of any army that is heavily invested into ground units, as they can steal them and turn them against their owner if given the chance. Spells and abilities urse (Autocast) left Curses a target enemy unit, causing it to have a 33% chance to miss on an attack.

# Getting Sylvanas in Banshee Form

In **Warcraft III**, you obtain Sylvanas in her banshee form during **The Fall of Silvermoon** campaign mission. Originally, you needed to complete an optional quest to unlock her, but in the Reforged version, you only need to reach the gates of Silvermoon to defeat her and gain access to her as a unit. Once you do, you also unlock the ability to build fully upgraded Banshees from your structures.

## Her Abilities

Sylvanas in banshee form comes equipped with **Curse** as her starting spell, which causes a target enemy unit to have a 33% chance to miss on attacks. She can also learn **Anti-Magic Shell**, which creates a protective barrier that grants a target unit spell immunity for 90 seconds at a cost of 50 mana.

**Note:** In Reforged, while Sylvanas appears as a banshee unit mechanically, her appearance was modified to reflect her Dark Ranger form with some spell adjustments—her Black Arrow was replaced with Howl of Terror, and she uses a banshee missile instead of a Dark Ranger arrow.

original.question: What happened to the original Harvester of Wrath, and who has the title now?

According to the Curator, the original Harvester of Wrath still exists but hasn't been seen for 'a few epochs' and her whereabouts are unknown.

Long ago, her power was stripped from her and given to the Stonewright,Curator's Addendum who has held the title of Harvester of Wrath ever since.Medallion of Wrath (quest): "Therefore, as Harvester of Wrath, the second of that title [...]" According to the Curator, the first Harvester of Wrath still exists, but she hasn't been seen for "a few epochs" and her whereabouts are unknown. Harvester of Wrath: If we cross this bridge on foot, defeating any who would dare stop us, we may have an audience with the mother of the stoneborn. However, know that our return safety is not guaranteed, so try not to offend her. I do not look forward to this. Rewards You will receive: 9,450 XP Progress We must be extra cautious. Completion I remember when the first stoneborn moved with the breath of anima infused by the Stonewright. Never, ever, confuse her sympathy for her creations as a weakness. She holds the Medallion of Wrath for a reason. Notes Guardians of Wrath dot the sides of the path along the bridge. Though they're initially still, a couple of them will animate and attack as you pass by. Halfway across the bridge, First Talon Syndaal drops from the sky to challenge you. As Syndaal reaches low health: Continue to the end of the bridge. Down the steps at Dominance Keep, the Stonewright awaits. They appear , but they will not attack unless provoked. Speak to the Stonewright: You curtailed your rage enough to cross the bridge despite those who stood in your way. This is progress. original.response:
# The Harvesters of Wrath

The **original Harvester of Wrath** was one of the venthyr Harvesters created directly by Sire Denathrius in ancient times. However, her power was stripped away and transferred to the **Stonewright**, who has held the title ever since. According to the Curator, the original Harvester still exists somewhere, but she hasn't been seen for "a few epochs" and her current whereabouts remain unknown.

The **Stonewright** now serves as the second Harvester of Wrath and holds considerable authority in Revendreth. She's responsible for the stoneborn and is depicted as a formidable figure—the Curator warns that her sympathy for her creations should never be mistaken for weakness. During the Chains of Domination campaign, the Stonewright even offered her Medallion of Wrath to the Maw Walker to help empower the other Harvesters against Denathrius's potential return.

original.question: Who is Lei Shen and what did he do in World of Warcraft?
original.reference: Lei Shen, also known as the Thunder King, was the first emperor of the mogu and the most powerful warlord in mogu history. After stealing the power of storms from the titan keeper Ra, he united the mogu clans into the first great mogu empire and made significant contributions to Pandaria, most notably constructing the Serpent's Spine barrier to separate the mantid from the rest of the Empire. In recent times, he was resurrected by Zandalari allies and attempted to unify the mogu again, but was defeated by a combined assault from the Shado-Pan, Alliance, and Horde on the Isle of Thunder.
snippets: Lei Shen: It is said that this early rivalry was instrumental in developing Lei Shen's thirst for power.Sparkmancer Vu epitaph Despite seeing the mogu's infighting and squabbling as a betrayal of their great potential, Lei Shen remained a stalwart vassal of his father. A close advisor eventually betrayed and murdered Lei Shen's father, causing the clan to fall apart and seek refuge with other warlords, leaving Lei Shen and a handful of loyal guardsmen behind. Rather than continue the fighting, the young mogu chose to go into exile, meditating and wandering the land. Most mogu saw Lei Shen's meditations as a sign of madness and proof that he would never threaten any mogu ever again. The power of Ra-den Like his brothers, Lei Shen was strong of arm and fluent in the ways of war. But unlike others of his generation, he spoke of more than conquest. The legends of his forefathers were etched into his heart. Once Lei Shen had come of age and commanded his own legions, he announced his intent to awaken the gods. After years of searching, the warlord found the entrance to the hidden vaults beneath the lands north of the Vale of Eternal Blossoms. What he found therein was illuminating: the titanic watcher Ra, who stood guard over a powerful titan device, the Engine of Nalak'sha.The Characters of Warcraft/Lei Shen The Highkeeper showed no reaction upon seeing Lei Shen, even as the mogu began asking him questions about the mogu's purpose.

Lei Shen (object): thumb|Lei Shen Lei Shen is a mogu lore object found at on the northeastern side of Dawnseeker Promontory on the west coast of the Isle of Thunder. Interacting with the lore object is one of the requirements of the Pandaria exploration achievement Rumbles of Thunder. Patch changes External links Category:Isle of Thunder objects Category:Readable objects es:Lei Shen (objeto)

Lei Shen (tactics): Lei Shen is the twelfth and final boss encounter in the Throne of Thunder on Looking for Raid and Normal Mode. Background Adventure Guide Tyrant. Dictator. Conqueror. Emperor. These titles of the Thunder King were earned by bringing absolute misery to every race of Pandaria. Resurrected into a world where his people no longer rule, Lei Shen is bent on ending the farce and bringing the land under his heel once more. Encounter design The core concept for the Lei Shen fight developed during a brainstorming session as members of the encounter design team were throwing around ideas for potential fight mechanics, trying to see if anything would stick. A popular idea was for him to need to pause and recharge periodically, but someone suggested an interesting twist on that idea—the Thunder King isn't just a battery that needs to recharge; he's actually a power source himself. So instead of him drawing power from his citadel, the citadel draws power from him. He is a mighty warrior in his own right, with distinctive weapon and lightning attacks, but his mere presence energizes and activates portions of his citadel’s defenses, and managing those becomes a central element of the encounter.

Foot of Lei Shen: thumb|Foot of Lei Shen. The Foot of Lei Shen is the last step before reaching the Throne of Thunder on the northern part of the Isle of Thunder. The area is unlocked after completing The Fall of Shan Bu, which finishes The Fall of Shan Bu stage of the Isle of Thunder storyline. original.response:
# Lei Shen: The Thunder King

**Lei Shen was a mogu warlord who became the tyrannical Emperor of Pandaria**, earning titles like Tyrant, Dictator, Conqueror, and the Thunder King. He's one of World of Warcraft's most significant antagonists, featured as the final boss of the Throne of Thunder raid in the Mists of Pandaria expansion.

Early in his life, Lei Shen witnessed his father's murder at the hands of a traitorous advisor, which fractured his clan and forced him into exile. Rather than continue fighting, the young mogu withdrew to meditate—a choice most other mogu dismissed as madness. However, this period of reflection transformed him. Unlike his peers who only spoke of conquest, Lei Shen harbored grander ambitions rooted in mogu legend: **he sought to awaken the gods themselves**. After years of searching, he discovered the hidden vaults beneath northern Pandaria and found the titanic watcher Ra guarding the Engine of Nalak'sha, a powerful titan device. With this knowledge and artifact, Lei Shen rose to dominate all of Pandaria, bringing absolute misery to every race on the continent.

When players encounter him in the Throne of Thunder, Lei Shen has been resurrected into a world where the mogu no longer rule, and he's determined to reclaim his dominance and restore his people's supremacy over the land.

original.response:
**Yol'Tithian** was a Highborne sorcerer who played a crucial role in one of the most pivotal moments of the War of the Ancients. When the conflict reached its catastrophic conclusion and the night elves faced total annihilation, Yol'Tithian assisted **Dath'Remar Sunstrider** in orchestrating the escape from Zin-Azshari, the capital city that had become ground zero for the Burning Legion's invasion.

His involvement in this escape was significant because Dath'Remar and his followers would go on to become the **High elves** after they were exiled from night elf society for their magical practices. Yol'Tithian's sorcerous abilities made him valuable to this desperate flight, helping to ensure that at least some of the Highborne survived the catastrophe that saw the Well of Eternity destroyed and the world sundered.

original.question: What is the Wanderer's Way philosophy that pandaren follow, and who are some examples of characters that practice it?
original.reference: The Wanderer's Way is a pandaren philosophy that teaches followers to take each journey one step at a time, observe everything around them, speak to everyone they meet, and soak in all the details, while also knowing when to explore and when to stand and fight. Notable practitioners of this philosophy include Liu Lang, Chen Stormstout, and Li Li Stormstout.

They are a rare breed, on the whole far more adventurous than their land-locked and long-lost relatives on mainland Pandaria. When the great turtle is threatened, and their whole wandering homeland is in jeopardy of being swallowed by the sea, young heroes had to accelerate their training and spring into action. Mists of Pandaria Following the events of the Wandering Isle storyline the Huojin and Tushui pandaren of the Wandering Isle made their way to the Horde and Alliance capitals, respectively, to pledge their allegiance to their new 'Emperor'. Having each joined one of the two major factions, the respective pandaren groups began to train their new allies in the ways of the monk. As well as residing in the factions' capital cities, individual monk trainers traveled to areas such as the Valley of Trials and Northshire Abbey to train young members of each of their faction's races.

Tushui Pandaren: The Tushui Pandaren, often shortened simply to the Tushui,Aysa Cloudsinger#Quotes are a group of Alliance pandaren led by Master Aysa Cloudsinger that adhere to the path of the Tushui, one of the two primary philosophies among the pandaren of the Wandering Isle. Tushui teachings encourage living a venerable life through meditation, rigorous training, and moral conviction. When the Wandering Isle was opened to the rest of the world, pandaren who upheld the tenets of Tushui were drawn to the Alliance due to their similar philosophies. Since then, they have taken part in many conflicts and events throughout Azeroth alongside their new allies. The Tushui pandaren are contemplative, focused on abstract ideals of justice and morality, and believe that a principled life is the only one worth living. Their idealogical opposites are the Huojin Pandaren, also of the Wandering Isle, but who chose to join with the Horde. Both groups represent the majority of pandaren within their respective factions. At this time, the group was headed by Aysa Cloudsinger, a stalwart and gifted monk, and coexisted in relative harmony with an opposing philosophy in the form of the Huojin.
original.response:
# The Wanderer's Way

The **Wanderer's Way** is a pandaren philosophy that emphasizes embracing curiosity and adventure while remaining grounded in principle. It teaches practitioners to **take each journey one step at a time, observe everything around you, speak to everyone you meet, and soak in all the details**. Importantly, it also stresses knowing when to explore and when to stand your ground and fight—balancing wanderlust with wisdom.

This philosophy originates from the wanderlust-stricken pandaren of the Wandering Isle and is deeply connected to **Liu Lang**, the legendary first explorer who ventured beyond Pandaria on the back of a giant turtle. His spirit of adventure continues to inspire pandaren across generations, with many celebrating him during the **Wanderer's Festival** at Turtle Beach.

The philosophy is actively practiced by several notable characters. **Chen Stormstout**, a native of the Wandering Isle, exemplifies this adventurous spirit, as do **Li Li Stormstout** and **Liu Lang** himself. The pandaren of the Wandering Isle in general tend to follow this path more than their mainland counterparts, and characters like **Gravewalker Gie** and **Mojo Stormstout** are believed to practice it as well. Even today, when pandaren are caught gazing off to the horizon lost in thought, others ask if they're **"waiting for the turtle"**—a reference to Liu Lang's legendary journeys.

original.question: What exactly is a cartel in World of Warcraft, and how are goblins organized into them?
original.reference: A cartel is a large group of traders in goblin and Broker society. Goblins are organized into cartels, which are the most powerful groups among goblin society. After goblins ended their conflicts with each other, they formed cartels as rival organizations that compete in trade and economics rather than warfare. These cartels are typically led by trade princes, though not exclusively.

Cartel: A cartel refers to a large group of traders in goblin and Broker society. Goblins After the goblins overthrew the Zandalari trolls, they celebrated their new found freedom by turning on each other, creating chaos as countless feuds were waged and alliances formed. Soon enough they were split into various different groups, with the most powerful of these known as the cartels. However, as the battles raged on and it became clear no one was actually winning, the cartels realized it was in their mutual interests to broker a truce. Despite this, the conflict would never really end, as the goblins now turned to trade, doing battle in the economic arena. For years to come the cartels were rivals, constantly outshining and outsmarting each other as they continued in their quest for wealth and power.World of Warcraft: Chronicle Volume 1, pg. 158 Goblin cartels often compete with other cartels, such as in the Trade Wars. These organizations are normally led by trade princes, but they don't have to be; for example, although the Venture Company is a cartel, its leader Mogul Razdunk is not a trade prince.Mogul Razdunk (tactics) Known cartels Bilgewater Cartel Steamwheedle Cartel Venture CompanyMorin Cloudstalker (quest) Unnamed neutral cartel led by Trade Prince DonaisAsk CDev Answers - Round 4 Brokers The Brokers of the Shadowlands are organized into various cartels.

Goblin: The goblins, having incidentally caused the destruction of their Town-In-A-Box with the volcanic eruption, return to Thrall and the orcs just in time to help stave off the Alliance assault. Impressed with the goblins cunning and strength the orcs agreed to take the fight to Trade Prince Gallywix, which had enslaved the majority of the members of the Bilgewater Cartel and put them to work mining resources. After disrupting Gallywix's operations, the free goblins overthrow Gallywix himself with the assistance of Thrall. Surrendering himself, Gallywix promises the Bilgewater Cartel will reform and become a part of the Horde. Thrall accepts, offering to send a representative to the Horde's new Warchief Garrosh Hellscream and promises them a new home in Azshara and a place within the Horde. The goblins of the Bilgewater Cartel now reside chiefly in Azshara, which they have massively altered. Goblins have also been seen assisting their allies in other areas such as Northern Barrens, Stonetalon Mountains, and Felwood. The goblins have made a home in Orgrimmar where Boss Mida runs the Goblin Slums an area of filth, pollution, and technology, just how the goblins like it.

Bilgewater Cartel: thumb|Banner of the cartel. The Bilgewater Cartel is one of the largest goblin organizations found on Azeroth. Run by the ruthless Trade Prince Gallywix, it is the most powerful trading company on Kezan. From their headquarters at Bilgewater Port, the cartel dominated the trade all over the South Seas,In-game reputation description until it was destroyed due to the Cataclysm. After the events in the Lost Isles, the Bilgewater joined the Horde and built a new capital in the Kalimdorian region of Azshara, Bilgewater Harbor. They compete with other goblin organizations such as the Steamwheedle Cartel and Venture Company over both resources and customers. In the game, the Bilgewater Cartel is a Horde-aligned faction related to the playable Bilgewater goblins, introduced in Cataclysm. It is thus the sixth racial Horde-faction to be added to the game. They have their own quartermaster, rewards, and tabard which in dungeons will boost reputation for every kill. History Originally the slaves of Zandalari trolls on the Isle of Kezan, the goblin race was forced to mine kaja'mite ore out of the volcanic bowels of Mount Kajaro. The trolls used this potent mineral for their voodoo rituals, but it had an unexpected effect on the slaves who were in constant contact with it: kaja'mite generated a startling new cunning and intelligence in the goblins.

Steamwheedle Cartel: The Steamwheedle Cartel is one of the largest and most successful of the goblin cartels in the Undermine. It is a neutral organization that controls several cities and towns around the world, and has allied themselves with a number of other groups such as the Blackwater Raiders in Booty Bay. The Steamwheedle Cartel has made a great deal of profit from maintaining neutrality in the conflict between the Horde and the Alliance. Their services are always available to the highest bidder, employing anyone regardless of their affiliation if they can produce results. Background Great Wars Prior to the First War,:File:Chronicle2 Eastern Kingdoms Before the First War.jpg the goblins of the Steamwheedle Cartel managed to drive out the trolls from a small port town inhabited by humans which had been overrun. They claimed control of the immediate area and the shores of the cove for their new center for operations in the Eastern Kingdoms — Booty Bay.Wharfmaster Dizzywig (Barrens)The Barrens Port: Most likely Velinde found a trading vessel in Ratchet to take her to Blackwater Cove in Azeroth.Passage to Booty Bay: Velinde. Booked passage to Booty Bay on the Black Osprey. During the war, the goblins witnessed the Old Horde's arrival and the conquest of Stormwind. With more conflicts on the horizon, they were determined to profit from it.
original.response:
# Goblin Cartels in World of Warcraft

In WoW, **a cartel is a large trading group in goblin society**—essentially massive commercial enterprises that dominate goblin economics and politics. After the goblins freed themselves from Zandalari troll rule, they initially descended into chaos with countless feuds and rivalries. When it became clear that endless warfare wasn't profitable, the most powerful goblin factions realized they'd make more money through trade than bloodshed. They brokered a truce and shifted their competition from military conflict to the economic arena, creating the cartel system.

**Cartels are typically led by trade princes**, though not always—the Venture Company is a cartel led by Mogul Razdunk, who isn't a trade prince. The major cartels include the **Bilgewater Cartel** (now Horde-aligned after the Cataclysm), the **Steamwheedle Cartel** (neutral and spread across multiple cities worldwide), and the **Venture Company**. These organizations constantly compete with each other in what goblins call the Trade Wars, constantly outsmarting and outshining one another in their endless quest for wealth and power.

The cartel system transformed goblin society from warring factions into a structured hierarchy of competing business interests, allowing them to maintain their fierce rivalry while actually turning a profit.

original.question: Who is Bolten Vanyst and what's his connection to Tyrathan Khort?
original.reference: Bolten Vanyst is the lord of the Vanyst family to whom Tyrathan Khort had sworn his service. Bolten directed Khort to lead a squad of scouts to the Serpent's Heart in Pandaria, but the mission resulted in an attack by the Sha that killed all scouts except Khort. Feeling guilt over the incident, Khort later believed Bolten used his nephew Morelan as a tool to further his own ambitions during the Pandaria campaign.
snippets: Bolten Vanyst: Bolten Vanyst is is the lord of the Vanyst family of whom Tyrathan Khort had sworn his service too. A vain man with a scheming harridan of a wife, Bolten is always for a great campaign as it takes him away from Stormwind. The father of three daughters, Bolten has seemingly promised his realm to one of their husbands should they be pleased to take up the mantle. Yet when Bolten leaves on a campaign, his nephew- Morelan Vanyst- is always left as regent. When the hidden continent of Pandaria was discovered, Bolten assisted the Alliance in the region. He directed Tyrathan Khort to lead a squad of scouts to the Serpent's Heart and expected them to report within three hours. However the scout force was attacked by the Sha and all but Khort were killed in the ensuing struggle. Driven by guilt, Khort limped away from the battlefield and was eventually taken to the Shado-Pan Monastery to heal by Chen Stormstout. Feeling the need to prove himself after Tyrathan Khort seemingly passed away, Morelan Vanyst journeyed to join the Pandaria campaign where Khort surmises that Bolten was quick to use his nephew as a tool to further his own ambitions.Vol'jin: Shadows of the Horde References Category:Human characters Category:Aristocrats Category:Stormwind (kingdom) Category:Lore characters Category:Vol'jin: Shadows of the Horde characters es:Bolten Vanyst

Tyrathan Khort: Tyrathan Khort is a human hunter in the Alliance army, but not especially high ranking. He was found by Chen Stormstout after being injured in battle and brought to the Shado-Pan Monastery to heal, where the Shado-Pan had him care for the recovering Vol'jin so their opposite personalities would achieve balance. Tyrathan was haunted by doubt after losing his scouting squad to a sha attack in the Jade Forest. He and Vol'jin eventually become friends, even making a pact to kill whomever killed the other. Biography As a boy, Tyrathan was inspired to become a hunter by the legends of the Unseen Path.Tyrathan's gossip text The Khort family has long served the Vanyst family of Stormwind nobles, a tradition which Tyrathan continued. Tyrathan met his wife when he was hunting a pack of wolves, but as time went on his wife grew to worry about how easily killing came to Tyrathan. He feels that while he was away as a soldier, that perhaps his wife and Morelan Vanyst have grown close. Tyrathan served in Daelin Proudmoore's forces that occupied Theramore shortly after the Third War and during that time he met the troll leader, Vol'jin. At some point in the past he spent time in Stranglethorn Vale and learned how to speak Zandali from his troll guide Keren'dal. He speaks Zandali with a Stranglethorn accent. Pandaria Tyrathan's superior, Bolten Vanyst, had him lead a squad of scouts to Serpent's Heart and report back within three hours.

Morelan Vanyst: Morelan Vanyst, the nephew of Bolten Vanyst, is left as the regent lord of the Vanyst family when his uncle leaves on a campaign. Having lost his wife when she gave birth to their son, Morelan would become close with the wife of Tyrathan Khort as their children played together. Hearing and believing of Khort's passing in Pandaria, Morelan would join the Pandaria campaign in order to prove that he could be brave. His uncle, ever a schemer, was quick to use his nephew to further his ambitions. After Tyrathan Khort's efforts against the Zandalari, Khal'ak ordered Captain Nir'zan to discover his identity by attacking the Alliance and taking human prisoners. Morelan and a group of fellow soldiers were subsequently attacked and only the timely intervention of Khort and his allies rescued him from captivity. Hoping and yet fearing, Morelan demanded to know where Tyrathan Khort was; pointing out he would recognize those arrows anywhere and did not believe Vol'jin when he said that there was no Tyrathan Khort. After Vol'jin claimed to have killed Khort, Morelan paled while mentioning the rumors of Vol'jin's death.

Keren'dal: Keren'dal is a jungle troll who served as a guide to Tyrathan Khort during his time in Stranglethorn. Over time Khort learned Zandali from Keren'dal, though at first it was without the troll realizing it. After Khort's time in Stranglethorn was over, the two parted ways; as far as Khort knows, Keren'dal is still alive.Shadows of the Horde, chapter 9 References Category:Jungle troll characters Category:Lore characters Category:Vol'jin: Shadows of the Horde characters es:Keren'dal

Larsi: Larsi was a young hunter who answered the Alliance's call to journey to the newly discovered continent of Pandaria. Meeting Tyrathan Khort on the boat right there, Larsi thanked Khort and admitted that she thought the Pandaria campaign was going to be an adventure. Always there should Khort need something, Khort and Larsi struck up a friendship, though Tyrathan got the sense that she felt that she owed him something for some unknown reason. Larsi was killed by the sha while scouting the Serpent's Heart at the command of Bolten Vanyst. Khort, as the only survivor of the sha attack, was wracked with guilt over her death.Vol'jin: Shadows of the Horde References Category:Human characters Category:Hunter characters Category:Lore characters Category:Vol'jin: Shadows of the Horde characters Category:Deceased characters es:Larsi
original.response:
**Bolten Vanyst is a Stormwind noble and lord of the Vanyst family**, known for being vain and ambitious. He's a schemer who prefers to be away on military campaigns rather than dealing with his domestic life—he has a wife described as manipulative and three daughters. He often leaves his nephew Morelan as regent when he departs.

**Tyrathan Khort serves the Vanyst family as part of a long-standing tradition** that spans generations. Khort is a skilled hunter in the Alliance army, and Bolten directly commanded him during the Pandaria campaign. This is where their connection becomes tragic: Bolten ordered Khort to lead a scouting squad to Serpent's Heart with a three-hour deadline to report back. The mission went catastrophically wrong when the Sha attacked, killing everyone in the squad except Khort, who barely survived and was rescued by Chen Stormstout.

The incident haunted Khort with guilt over losing his squad, including a young hunter named Larsi. While Khort recovered at the Shado-Pan Monastery, Bolten back in Stormwind used the situation to his advantage—when Khort was believed dead, Bolten quickly deployed his nephew Morelan to the Pandaria campaign, cynically leveraging the young man's desire to prove himself to further his own political ambitions.

original.question: What is twinking in WoW?
original.reference: Twinking is the practice of equipping a low-level character with the best possible gear available at their level bracket to dominate in PvP. Twinks typically lock their experience gain to stay in a specific bracket and use rare drops, crafted items, and enchants that are unusually powerful for their level.
snippets: WoW Token: Buy and sell demo The following gallery of images demonstrates the process of buying the WoW Token from the In-Game Store and selling it on the Auction House, as seen on the public test realm: The following gallery of images demonstrates the process of purchasing a WoW Token with gold on the public test realm and redeeming it for game time. WoW Tokens can also be purchased or redeemed from the character select screen, to reactivate a currently inactive account. From the Auction House When you purchase a token from the Auction House, you will receive a letter from the Auction House with the token. Overview video FAQ The Basics How do I buy a WoW Token for real money? WoW Tokens will be available for purchase for real money through the World of Warcraft in-game Shop. You can access the Shop through the row of feature buttons next to your character's bags. I need gold! How do I sell a WoW Token to another player? You'll be able to sell WoW Tokens through a dedicated Token exchange in the Auction House, located in a new Game Time section. WoW Tokens cannot be traded or sold any other way. How much gold will I receive when I sell a WoW Token? The gold value of a Token will be determined dynamically based on supply and demand. When you put a Token up for sale, you'll be quoted the amount of gold you'll receive upon a successful sale.

original.response:
**Twinking** is the practice of equipping a low-level character with high-quality gear to make them extremely powerful compared to other players at that same level. Twinks are typically used in PvP battlegrounds where they dominate opponents who are wearing standard leveling gear.

Players who twink their characters hunt for rare world drops and dungeon loot specifically optimized for low-level play—items like the Sunscale Helmet for level 49 warriors, the Sentry Cloak, and the Staff of the Friar. These pieces give twinks a massive advantage in stats and survivability compared to normally-geared players, allowing them to control battlegrounds and secure kills much more easily.

original.question: What is the difference between Horde and Alliance?
original.reference: The Horde and Alliance are the two main factions in World of Warcraft. The Alliance is composed of races such as humans, dwarves, gnomes, night elves, draenei, and worgen, and is based in Stormwind. The Horde is composed of races such as orcs, trolls, tauren, undead, blood elves, and goblins, and is based in Orgrimmar. The two factions are in constant conflict throughout the game.

Horde: This led to a temporary unity between the two factions when Varok asked Anduin Wrynn, High King of the Alliance, for aid in assaulting Orgrimmar to end both the reign of Sylvanas and the enmity between the Horde and Alliance. Ultimately, the rebellion caused the Horde to lose its top leadership when Sylvanas killed Varok and abandoned the Horde. With his death and her abandonment, the Fourth War is considered over and the two factions have begun talks of peace, though many surmise it may not last or cannot forgive the atrocities of the past.Warchief of the Horde (quest)The Price of Peace Currently the Horde and Alliance are in an "uneasy armistice". The Horde is one of the two factions that player characters belong to―the Alliance being the other―as determined by a given character's race (with the exception of Pandaren which can be both). Besides race, being a member of the Horde or Alliance affects gameplay through what settlements, NPCs, quests, reputation factions and in some cases items and encounters the player may access. Faction mechanics also prevent Horde and Alliance players from talking with each other or making groups together, and most PvP gameplay involves Horde players fighting against Alliance players. The divide between the Horde and Alliance—in regard to gameplay mechanics, story elements, and sometimes in social aspects like faction pride—acts as the core element of the World of Warcraft experience.
original.response:
# Horde vs Alliance: The Core Divide

The **Horde and Alliance are the two opposing factions** that define World of Warcraft's entire experience. When you create a character, your race determines which faction you belong to—the Alliance includes humans, dwarves, and draenei, while the Horde includes orcs, tauren, and blood elves (Pandaren can join either faction).

This factional divide shapes virtually everything about your gameplay. You'll access different settlements, NPCs, quests, and reputation factions depending on which side you're on. The mechanics even prevent players from different factions from communicating or grouping together, and most PvP content pits Horde players directly against Alliance players. It's not just flavor—it's woven into how the game functions.

Lore-wise, the two factions have been at war throughout much of WoW's history, though their relationship has evolved. After the Fourth War and Sylvanas's betrayal of the Horde, the factions reached an "uneasy armistice" and began peace talks, though many wounds remain from past atrocities. **The fundamental tension between Horde and Alliance serves as the core element of the World of Warcraft experience itself**—whether you're engaging with story, PvP, or just choosing where your character calls home.

original.question: What's the deal with Iron Qon in Throne of Thunder? Does he have different phases or is it just a straight DPS fight?
original.reference: Iron Qon is not a straight DPS fight — it is a four-phase encounter where Qon switches between different magical quilen champions, each empowered with vicious elemental attacks. This creates four separate intense fights rolled into one, rather than a simple tank-and-spank. The overall fight involves tank-and-spank mechanics combined with environment awareness mechanics.
snippets: Iron Qon: Iron Qon is the tenth boss encounter in the Throne of Thunder. It is a tank and spank with numerous environment awareness mechanics. Background Adventure Guide Legends speak of the brutality of Qon and his quilen champions. The fearless mogu commander was dubbed "Iron Qon" by the Thunder King for his unrelenting resolve to claim victory after victory for the empire regardless of the cost. Encounter Design Qon is one of Lei Shen's elite personal guard, and a master of mounted combat. An original concept for this fight was inspired by the Three Kingdoms warlord Lu Bu, a fierce warrior renowned for his skill in fighting with a spear from horseback. Though Qon does have some very cool and unique mounted attack animations, in the end we found it more interesting to explore gameplay via the different abilities of his mounts instead. Thus, the encounter involves Qon switching among different magical quilen, each of which is empowered with vicious elemental attacks, creating a four-phase encounter that is almost like four separate intense fights rolled into one.Patch 5.2 Raid Preview: Throne of Thunder Spells and abilities The Quilen Champions - Iron Qon battles atop his finest Quilen champions.

Primordius: Primordius is the eighth boss encounter in the Throne of Thunder. The fight is a relatively simple DPS race compared to the rest of the fights in the Throne of Thunder. Background Adventure Guide Deep within the bowels of Lei Shen's island citadel, mogu flesh-shapers formed the saurok out of a magical substance of unknown origin. When the citadel was abandoned, some experiments languished in the dark pools for far too long... Encounter Design The fact that the mogu created the saurok (among other creatures native to Pandaria) is something that may not have been obvious to players who were exploring the continent and learning about its inhabitants. This encounter provided us with a chance to "show, not tell" this piece of Pandaria's history. The actual mechanics of the fight evolved from discussion and brainstorming around the themes of evolution and mutation; a few of us had also recently watched the movie Prometheus, and the vaults of black goo in that movie helped inspire the overall vibe of Primordius’ lair.Patch 5.2 Raid Preview: Throne of Thunder Spells and abilities Mutation: Ventral Sacs - Sacs located around Primordius's frill will periodically erupt, inflicting 20,000 Nature damage to all players every 1 sec. Mutation: Gas Bladder - A volatile mutation that allows Primordius to spray Caustic Gas, inflicting 4,050,000 Nature damage split evenly between all players within a 18-yard cone.
Qon's Flaming Scimitar: Source This item drops from Iron Qon in Throne of Thunder. Patch changes Category:Throne of Thunder items
Iron Qon's Boot Knife: Source This item drops from Iron Qon in Throne of Thunder. Patch changes Category:Throne of Thunder items
Dam'ren's Frozen Footguards: Source This item drops from Iron Qon in Throne of Thunder. Patch changes Category:Throne of Thunder items
original.response:
**Iron Qon is actually a four-phase fight**, which makes it way more dynamic than a typical tank-and-spank. The key to the encounter is that Qon cycles through different magical quilen mounts, and each one brings its own elemental attacks and mechanics to the fight.

The encounter was originally inspired by the historical figure Lu Bu, a legendary warrior known for devastating spear attacks from horseback. The developers initially wanted to explore that mounted combat theme, but ultimately decided it was more interesting to give each quilen its own distinct abilities rather than just focusing on Qon's personal moves. So instead of fighting the same boss four times, you're essentially dealing with four separate intense fights rolled into one as he switches mounts.

As for mechanics, it's not a pure DPS race—there's definitely environmental awareness required. You'll need to adapt to whatever each quilen throws at you while maintaining damage output. So it's got more teeth to it than just burning him down, but you're primarily focused on dealing with the changing threat each mount presents rather than executing complex puzzle mechanics.
